author: @robinhooper7702
text: It is my understanding that Maglev is based in a horizontal figure 8.  The magnetic assembly induces a current that opposes the magnetic pole orientation. This induces a current in the opposite direction causing a negative field in the other half of the 8 ,which propels the vehicle.

author: @MrBrelindm
text:
Induction happens whenever there is relative motion between magnetic lines of force and a diamagnetic conductor material. This is how teluric currents are produced from the solar wind and from CMEs which strike the planet. These teluric currents can be quite strong depending upon ground composition and moisture content and can (and have)  caused transformers to blow up, short out and catch fire causing blackouts. 

These CMEs are magnetically polarized but a glancing blow would only result in one polarity of magnetism striking the planet and teluric currents are still produced. How strong can these currents get? Read up on the 1857 Carrington Event and you'll know that telegraph lines worked even after the batteries were disconnected! Telegraph operators got shocked - sometimes severely so. Some telegraph lines actually arced to the ground! Some Telegraph stations even caught fire!

author: @TheAndreasMustola
text:
How about generating electricity using existing surfaces in your home? 
I live in a flat with concrete ceiling. So in theory you could use the ceiling as a giant heat sink for a thermal generator. The house itself shake with the wind and pick up noise and footsteps so that could be used for vibration harvesting generator. So it would be interesting to create a device that could be placed in the ceiling and harvest this energy.

author: @magabesse
text: Sometimes RMS ADHD prevents him from noticing things. He just recreated an old patent for a brushless, homopolar generator/motor. If Rob paid attention to the right/left hand rule for current in motors/generators, he would have placed the coil vertically between the magnets and gotten much higher output. Also he would have noticed that he's outputting DC power, (pulsed because of a damaged magnet). So you can get rid of rectifiers. The maglev part and turbine are Rob's awesome improvement.

author: @OpeyCorp
text: You should use separate magnetic fields for the levitation and generation. You could add a flat metal disc to the top to shunt the south field, then fasten Neodymium bar mags to the top of that in a N,S,N,S config, flip the turbine over because its orientation is irrelevant, the add the coil to the very top.. I know part of the point was simplicity, but you'll generate more efficiently with alternating NS than just a rotating north. I'd love to see giant Maglev wind turbines.

author: @HostileRespite
text: If the copper coil was insulated, made waterproof, could it be used underwater? Also, does the spinning cause a slow down after applying the coil? Just some of my questions.

replies.author: @magabesse
replies.text: if you waterproof your coils and magnets, to avoid corrosion and shorts, sure it can be used underwater. The spinning will slow down when you extract power, just like any other generator. Plus liquids create more drag than air.

author: @talon04091
text: Do you get a current in the bearings and how does it effect them?

replies.author: @fesmoas1692
replies.text:
You can measure the current by scope. IF you have any current, it destroys metal ball bearings in the long run by detrimental arc discharges through the ball bearing ring and balls, making the surface microscopically rough and changing the metal crystal structure of the hardened surfaces. 

Isolate all bearings, and do a discharge brush elsewhere. Or ask Rob to invent a tribologically performing conductive grease, as the research in ball bearing industry did. They do rarely use it though, because you will sell much less bearings :)

My question on this grease is: can you actually use it to transfer power without a brushed contact?
If I read Rob correctly, carbon Nano particle so are just for this job, and graphite even offers “emergency bearing” properties against blocking of bearings run hot. Question is: can you make a version running really low on viscosity losses?

Motivation for me:
Do wind power without inverter:

 I needed a semi-brushless three phase 400V AC synchronous generator for my wind energy plans, offering direct feed to grid or island grid constituting properties (together with cheap variable transmission drive chains regulating in effect the grid frequency). 

I want to modulate the power by having also a rotor coil - or a variable distance gap to the permanent magnets by truncated cone design. Have you seen a brushless using a cone coil design?

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
You can spin a magnet, but you can't spin a symmetric magnetic field in the axis of the direction of the magnetic field.  There is great deal  of research done on this topic.  Some of which lead to faraday paradox. Tangent: One can still generate electricity with a homopolar generator in a rotating disk.

One can veryify my first comment with a current carrying coil if one spins that coil in the axis of the magnetic field generated. Placing a stationary coil of any size in any orientation and any position around the coil rotating about the axis of the direction of its magnetic field.

Rob, in your setup, there's likely to be an asymmetry in the magnetic field, but that's likely to be too small to account for the voltages generated.  I believe it's more probable that when placing the turbine on the magnet it was not exactly centered so its mass creating a wobble in the magnet with the freqency of rotation.

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text:
The asymmetric part is an interesting point,,, perhaps you could build one,,, taking this into account, make a video about it?!
I'd be honoured to watch this as I do enjoy watching humans progress!!

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @AndreaDingbattSo every magnet has some tiny amount of asymmetry in their magnetic field. Some worse than others. But it's unlikely to be significant enough to generate the voltages seen. More likely the voltages generated were probably due to not balancing the weight of turbine with the weight of the magnet. One can rewatch the video at starting at 2:05, and see and hear significant vibrations which would most certainly create a time varying magnetic field that could induce a voltage in a stationary coil.

author: @kilokilos
text: I suspect there is a logerithmic like limit to the amount of weight such a maglev device can handle before it breaks apart, making it quite unsustainable?

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
It only makes it unsustainable if you don't have the good sense to;

1) Test how much weight it takes to over come the repulsion of the two magnets.
2) Insure that the weight of any disc placed on the upper magnet, does not exceed 50% of the weight obtained in step 1.

You could go to 75%, however, the forces that come into play when spinning a disc may cause the disc itself to disintegrate should the material not be able to handle those forces.

replies.author: @kilokilos
replies.text: @scotttovey magnets are inherently brittle, no amount of planning helps for that

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@kilokilos
Not all magnets are brittle. 
Some are actually flexible.

Brittle only comes into play when objects strike one another. 
A brittle object will break much easier than an object that has more give within it's structure and therefore will not necessarily hold up in certain situations.

In a magLev environment, the magnets are not to come in contact with each other. Should they come into contact, the frictionless design will be forfeit and the device that is using the magLev as a bearing, will become less efficient.

replies.author: @kilokilos
replies.text: @scotttovey they do not have to touch theoretically they never can, but the physical forces involved disintegrate the magnets very quickly, especially neodebium

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@kilokilos 
"they do not have to touch theoretically they never can, but the physical forces involved disintegrate the magnets very quickly, especially neodebium"

Theory is not proven fact. It is not proven science.

Theory is a supposition that one develops for a worse case scenario and for the purposes of testing in order to insure that the use of the material, will stand up in the conditions that it is placed in.

If the theory has not been tested and proven out, then the theory cannot be used to argue that a given outcome will occur when a given use scenario is implemented.

You say that in theory the magnets will disintegrate very quickly.
OK fine, either conduct an experiment and produce a video in which magnets, in the configuration of a magLev, actually disintegrate from the weight that is placed upon them, or provide a link to a video in which someone else has proven that the configuration leads to a failure.

replies.text: I would think that the magnetic field is moving like ribbons around the magnets .when you spin one you are moving the magnetic ribbons paste the coil.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @rayg436 Magnetic field lines don't exist. Their distances between eachother is  metaphor to represent field intensity but the metaphor shouldn't be  taken as literal lines. The are about as real as topological markings on a map. But where distance between lines on a topological lines in a map indicate steepness they represent magnetic field intensity.  More fake magnetic field lines simply represents a stronger field in that region.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
